coredata.dc:title: Dual co-doped fluorescent carbon dots for ultra-sensitive detection of Co2+ ions: Applications in food samples, bioimaging, and environmental monitoring
coredata.dc:creator: Priyadharshini, Alagarsamy
Napoleon, Ayyakannu Arumugam
coredata.dc:description: Metal and heteroatom dual co-doped fluorescent (FL) carbon dots (CDs) have recently gained significant attention as optical sensors due to their tunable optical properties. In this study, we developed zinc (Zn) and nitrogen (N)/boron (B) co-doped carbon dots (Zn/NB-CDs) synthesized by using zinc chloride, tartaric acid, and pyrimidine-5-boronic acid, serving as precursors. The as-prepare Zn/NB-CDs were characterized using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques, including Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray Diffraction Spectroscopy (XRD), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). These analyses revealed that the Zn/NB-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescent properties, showing blue emission, along with a high quantum yield of 25.10 %. In addition, the morphological analysis showed that the Zn/NB-CDs were well-dispersed, spherical nano-sized particles with an average size of 3.0 ± 0.2 nm. Additionally, the Zn/NB-CDs demonstrated remarkable fluorescent stability across a wide range of pH values and in high-ionic-strength solutions, indicating their robustness under different conditions. As optical sensors, the Zn/NB-CDs displayed high selectivity towards cobalt (Co2+) ions, as significant enhancing of fluorescence was observed in their presence of other metal cations. Notably, the Zn/NB-CDs sensor achieved a low detection limit of 3.37 nM for Co2+ ions highlighting the high sensitivity. Importantly, this sensing platform has enabled the detection of Co2+ in figs, cabbage, and pista food samples with satisfactory recoveries up to 90 %. The Zn/NB-CDs were further used in live-cell imaging, successfully visualizing cells and demonstrating their potential for biomedical applications. Moreover, the Zn/NB-CDs sensor holds significant promise for environmental monitoring, particularly for detecting Co2+ ions in water samples. Its high selectivity, sensitivity, and biocompatibility make it an excellent candidate for detecting metal ion pollution in environmental and biomedical perspectives.

head.ce:title: Dual co-doped fluorescent carbon dots for ultra-sensitive detection of Co2+ ions: Applications in food samples, bioimaging, and environmental monitoring

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632014 Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632014, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal and heteroatom dual co-doped fluorescent (FL) carbon dots (CDs) have recently gained significant attention as optical sensors due to their tunable optical properties. In this study, we developed zinc (Zn) and nitrogen (N)/boron (B) co-doped carbon dots (Zn/NB-CDs) synthesized by using zinc chloride, tartaric acid, and pyrimidine-5-boronic acid, serving as precursors. The as-prepare Zn/NB-CDs were characterized using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques, including Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray Diffraction Spectroscopy (XRD), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). These analyses revealed that the Zn/NB-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescent properties, showing blue emission, along with a high quantum yield of 25.10 %. In addition, the morphological analysis showed that the Zn/NB-CDs were well-dispersed, spherical nano-sized particles with an average size of 3.0 ± 0.2 nm. Additionally, the Zn/NB-CDs demonstrated remarkable fluorescent stability across a wide range of pH values and in high-ionic-strength solutions, indicating their robustness under different conditions. As optical sensors, the Zn/NB-CDs displayed high selectivity towards cobalt (Co2+) ions, as significant enhancing of fluorescence was observed in their presence of other metal cations. Notably, the Zn/NB-CDs sensor achieved a low detection limit of 3.37 nM for Co2+ ions highlighting the high sensitivity. Importantly, this sensing platform has enabled the detection of Co2+ in figs, cabbage, and pista food samples with satisfactory recoveries up to 90 %. The Zn/NB-CDs were further used in live-cell imaging, successfully visualizing cells and demonstrating their potential for biomedical applications. Moreover, the Zn/NB-CDs sensor holds significant promise for environmental monitoring, particularly for detecting Co2+ ions in water samples. Its high selectivity, sensitivity, and biocompatibility make it an excellent candidate for detecting metal ion pollution in environmental and biomedical perspectives.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles are materials between 1 and 100 nm in diameter, made of different substances such as metals, metal oxides, polymers, and semiconductors, while properties change depending on size and composition [1]. Carbon dots (CDs) are a specific subcategory of carbon-based nanoparticles, in general smaller than 10 nm, with quasi-spherical morphology and surface functional groups including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine groups [2]. In contrast to conventional nanoparticles, CDs are synthesized by cost-effective, environmentally friendly processes such as hydrothermal, microwave or pyrolysis processes. CDs show intense, adjustable fluorescence with high photochemical stability and low toxicity and are therefore suitable for biomedical applications. Nanoparticles and CDs both find applications in drug delivery, sensing, imaging, and catalysis, but CDs have the advantages of better biocompatibility, water dispersibility, and green synthesis [3]. CDs also have some drawbacks such as reduced quantum yields and structural heterogeneity. Interestingly, CDs are efficient in the detection of cobalt ion (Co2+) owing to fluorescence enhancement on their presence, allowing for sensitive and specific detection.

One notable application of CDs is in the detection of cobalt ions (Co2+), where their strong fluorescence can be enhanced in the presence of these ions, allowing for precise detection at low concentrations [4]. Cobalt (Co2+) ions, being a trace element, plays an important role in human health as it is a constituent of vitamin B12 involved in nucleic acid synthesis, metabolism, and other physiological functions [5]. However, its long-term exposure or excessive intake causes diseases such as allergies, cardiovascular disorders, and thyroid disorders. With extensive industrial application in industries such as electroplating and dye making, improper treatment can lead to extensive environmental contamination [6]. Therefore, creating effective techniques for detecting Co2+ ions in water is very important for protecting the environment as well as human health. Although carbon dots in general are considered to be less toxic, the chemicals employed during their synthesis, including metal salts and organic solvents, might be environmentally harmful if not handled appropriately [7]. Thus, the right waste disposal must be ensured to prevent contamination. In addition, CDs are biocompatible, further studies are required to elucidate their long-term implications on human health and the environment, particularly for large-scale or in vivo applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of carbon-based nanomaterials that have garnered significant attention since their discovery in 2004, primarily due to their unique optical properties, chemical stability, and low toxicity. These nanoparticles, typically less than 10 nm in diameter, exhibit strong fluorescence, making them highly attractive for applications such as bioimaging, sensing, drug delivery, and catalysis [8]. CDs can be synthesized using various methods, including top-down approaches like laser ablation [9] and arc discharge [10], as well as bottom-up processes that involve carbonization of organic precursors like hydrothermal [11], solvothermal [12] and microwave methods [13]. Their surfaces can be easily functionalized with different molecules, enabling tailored properties for specific uses, such as enhancing biocompatibility or improving fluorescence [14]. Fluorescent CDs, in particular, are recognized for their remarkable photoluminescent properties, allowing them to emit different colors under varying excitation wavelengths. This makes them highly useful in fields like security ink, bioimaging, chemical sensing, and optoelectronics. In contrast to traditional semiconductor quantum dots, which often contain heavy metals, CDs are biocompatible and have a lower environmental impact, making them safer alternatives for medical and environmental applications [15]. This is especially important for in vivo imaging and medical diagnostics, where low toxicity is crucial. Additionally, the surface of CDs can be modified with various chemical groups to enhance water solubility, targeting capabilities, or interactions with specific biomolecules, further expanding their potential in biological systems. Their sensitivity to environmental changes also makes CDs highly effective for sensing applications, such as detection of metal ions, organic molecules, or environmental pollutants. CDs are being explored for their roles in photocatalysis and optoelectronics, where their ability to absorb and emit light is valuable for energy conversion and light-emitting devices [16].

Metal-doped carbon dots tend to show stronger fluorescence emission than heteroatom-doped ones mainly because of the effect of the dopant metal ions on their electronic structure [17]. Metal dopants enable more effective electronic distribution and energy transfer within the carbon dot matrix mainly because of the d–d transitions characteristic of most transition metals. These d–d transitions provide additional energy levels that can enhance radiative recombination processes, hence the overall fluorescence intensity [18]. In contrast, heteroatom-doped carbon dots, though capable of controlling optical properties via surface passivation and electronic modulation, tend to lack the ability for such large-scale electronic transitions, which may lead to relatively lower fluorescence emission. Also, the incorporation of metal ions during synthesis will cause slightly larger particle size and perhaps more varied morphologies in the final carbon dots since the metal atoms can influence nucleation and growth doping [19–21]. By comparison, heteroatom-doped carbon dots, employing lighter non-metallic atoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, or phosphorus, will possess smaller and more uniform sizes with nearly spherical morphologies due to the fact that these atoms can more easily insert into the carbon lattice. Metal doping generally not only imparts greater fluorescence but also changes the structural characteristics of carbon dots, thus making them different from heteroatom-doped counterparts both optically and physically structure [22–25]. This makes metal and heteroatom co-doped CDs ideal for applications in photocatalysis, sensing, bioimaging, and energy storage. Research has shown significant progress in developing functionalized CDs for specific applications. For example, Zhang et al. designed nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs for dual-mode fluorescent and colorimetric detection of Co2+ ions in real water samples [26]. Similarly, Kong et al. developed a sensitive and selective fluorescent probe using carbon dots for the detection of Co2+ ions. Their study demonstrated the efficacy of CDs synthesized via hydrothermal treatment in detecting Co2+ through fluorescence quenching mechanisms [27]. Regardless of the extensive application of carbon dots (CDs) in metal ion sensing, there are some essential challenges yet to be overcome, especially for the selective and sensitive detection of cobalt ions (Co2+). Poor selectivity for many CD-based sensors is one serious limitation, where Co2+ tends to display very similar coordination properties as other transition metal ions, and discrimination is challenging in their absence. Moreover, most current systems are based on fluorescence quenching mechanisms that are vulnerable to background interference and signal attenuation, and fewer “turn-on” sensors with a clearer and more sensitive response are available. Moreover, sensing performance may be disrupted in real-world matrices like environmental water or biological fluids, where other ions or organic molecules can occlude active sites or disrupt the surface charge of CDs. To address such problems, the present work reports Zn/NB-CDs with superior surface functionality, better fluorescence properties, and a selective turn-on response to Co2+ ions, providing an alternative solution to such prevailing shortcomings.

Zinc-doped carbon dots, for instance, demonstrate enhanced fluorescence and photostability, rendering them ideal for biological visualization and diagnostic applications. The zinc doping process allows tunable emission wavelengths between 400 and 600 nm, depending on the concentration of zinc [28]. Additionally, zinc-doped CDs show improved electrical conductivity and catalytic activity, expanding their applications to optoelectronics and catalysis. Despite these advanced properties, their biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity are retained, making them versatile platforms for a wide range of uses. The unique fluorescence modulation by zinc ions, along with pH-dependent properties, further enhances their potential in sensing, providing a promising avenue for researchers and industries alike. Tammina et al. developed a nitrogen and zinc co-doped carbon dots (N, Zn-CDs) were synthesized via a one-step microwave digestion method, achieving a high photoluminescent quantum yield of 63.28 %. The N, Zn-CDs were characterized using various techniques and demonstrated excellent sensitivity for Fe3+ detection with a detection limit of 0.027 μM. The sensing system was successfully applied to real water samples, highlighting its potential for environmental monitoring applications [29]. Xu et al. reported zinc-doped carbon dots (Zn-CDs) with a quantum yield of up to 35 %, where zinc ions enhance fluorescence by surface passivation and reducing π–π stacking. Structural analyses and simulations clarified the effects of zinc doping. The Zn-CDs demonstrated ultra-trace detection of Hg2+ with a detection limit of 0.1 μM, showing promise for in vivo sensing and phototherapy applications [30]. Herein we synthesize Zn/NB-CDs via a simple one-step hydrothermal method using tartaric acid, pyrimidine-5-boronic acid, and ZnCl2. The synthesized Zn/NB-CDs to characterize the structural, morphological, and elemental features by using FT-IR, XRD, TEM, and XPS techniques. In addition, evaluate the fluorescence properties and stability of the Zn/NB-CDs under varying pH and ionic strengths. Furthermore, Zn/NB-CDs to develop a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence-based method for Co2+ detection in environmental and food samples. Finally, we tested the biocompatibility and bioimaging potential of the Zn/NB-CDs and Zn/NB-CDs/Co2+ in living cells (see Scheme 1 ).

Zinc chloride and tartaric acid were purchased from Indian Mart Pvt. Ltd. Pyrimidine-5-boronic acid was bought from Avra Synthesis Pvt. Ltd. o-phosphoric acid, NaOH, and NaCl were purchased from Harish Pvt. Ltd. All metal ions and EDTA was bought from The National Scientific Suppliers. All the compounds used in the synthesis were of analytical grade and without further purification. Milli-Q water was used to prepare all solutions throughout the experiment.

The hydrothermal method has been extensively employed to synthesize metal-doped and heteroatom co-doped carbon dots (CDs), including zinc-doped with nitrogen and boron co-doped CDs (Zn/NB-CDs) [31]. In order to create a homogenous solution, 50 ml of distilled water are used to dissolve 1.51 g of tartaric acid, 0.648 g of zinc chloride, and 1.21 g of pyrimidine-5-boronic acid. After being moved to an autoclave lined with Teflon, the mixture is heated in an oven for 8 h at 180 °C. When the resultant brown liquid reaches room temperature, it is centrifuged for 3 h at 10,000 rpm. Following filtration, the supernatant is freeze-dried, which causes Zn/NB-CDs to develop. To further purify the Zn/NB-CDs, the solution was dialyzed for 24 h in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1,000 rpm, followed by filtration through a 0.22 µm syringe filter to remove large particles, aggregates, and insoluble residues. In order to describe these carbon dots, several techniques are used, including UV–Vis, FL, FT-IR, XRD, XPS, and HR-TEM.

For optical characterization, the UV–visible absorbance spectra were obtained using a HITACHI V-730 spectrometer, and fluorescence emission was measured using a JASCO FP 8200 spectrophotometer. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was used with a SHIMADZU IRAffinity-1 spectrophotometer to detect functional groups and chemical bonds within the sample. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out using a Bruker X8 Apex diffractometer to identify the crystalline structure of the Ni/NS-CDs. Raman spectroscopy was conducted to examine the SERS behaviors of substrates by a Raman (Horiba HR Evolution 800) spectrometer with laser wavelength 532 nm, laser power 48 mW, diffraction grid 1200 gr/mm, and spatial resolution 500 nm at the core research support center for natural products and medical materials. Surface and core elements were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a Thermo Scientific Multilab-2000 spectrometer employing an Al Kα monochromatized source. Sample preparation for HR-TEM images involved depositing an aqueous solution of Ni/NS-CDs on a carbon-coated copper grid and letting it dry at room temperature. The grid was then dried and analyzed using the JEOL JEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscope at 200 kV.

The quantum yield (QY) of Zn/NB-CDs was determined using a standard procedure, with quinine sulphate (QY = 54 %, dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4) serving as the reference material. To minimize reabsorption effects, the absorbance values at the excitation wavelength (370 nm) were maintained between 0.02 and 0.05 in a 10 mm fluorescence cuvette. The QY of Zn/NB-CDs was calculated using the following equation:

In this formula, ϕx represents the quantum yield of the sample, while ϕreference denotes the quantum yield of the reference sample. Areference and Asample correspond to the absorbance of the reference and the sample, respectively, and Isample and Ireference are the integrated emission areas for the sample and reference. The terms ηsample and ηreference indicate the refractive indices of the sample and reference, respectively. The fluorescence quantum yield was reported to be 25.10 % [32].

The selectivity of Zn/NB-CDs was evaluated in the presence of interfering cations, including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ce3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Sr2+, Zr2+, and Zn2+, under identical experimental conditions. A Zn/NB-CDs solution was used to detect each metal ion at a predetermined concentration of 10 μM. For the detection of Co2+ ions, a HEPES buffer solution was to prepare at room temperature. Specifically, the Zn/NB-CDs solution was to prepare by mixing 2.8 mL of HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) with 10 μL of an aqueous solution containing Zn/NB-CDs (50 μg/mL) in a quartz cuvette designed for spectrofluorometric analysis. To test selectivity, 10 μL of a stock solution with known concentrations of Co2+ ions (ranging from 0 to 10 μM) was added to the cuvette. The mixture was then shaken for 5 min to ensure uniformity. The prepare Zn/NB-CDs solution (50 μg/mL) was further tested with solutions containing various concentrations of other metal ions to assess its selectivity. Fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded within the wavelength range of 300–400 nm, with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

Water samples sourced from diverse origins, including tap, sea, and well water was intentionally contaminated with precise concentrations of Co2+ ions. To eliminate particulate impurities, the samples underwent filtration through Whatman filter paper. Subsequently, the samples were spiked with Co2+ ions at varying concentrations, spanning from 0 to 10 μM. After a 2-min equilibration period at ambient temperature, the samples fluorescence intensities were recorded upon addition of Co2+ solutions.

To assess the cytotoxicity and cellular uptake of zinc doped with nitrogen and boron-co-doped carbon dots (Zn/NB-CDs) on HCT-116 cancer cells, a detailed study was performed utilizing the MTT assay and confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). To evaluate the cytotoxicity of the synthesized zinc doped, nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots (Zn/NB-CDs), a MTT assay was performed using HCT-116 cells. The cells were seeded in cells were plated at a density of 5 × 103 in 96-well plates and incubated overnight at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2. After incubation, 100 µL of Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM), supplemented with streptomycin (100 µL/mL), penicillin (100 µL/mL), and 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS), was added to each well. The medium contained varying concentrations of Zn/NB-CDs (0–100 µg/mL) and the cells were incubated with the Zn/NB-CDs for an additional 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) to remove excess nanoparticles. Following this, 5 µg/mL of MTT solution was added to each well and incubated for 4 h. To solubilize the formazan crystals formed during the assay, 100 µL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well after the medium was evaporated. Cell viability (%) was calculated using the equation:
%

where ODcontrol represents the absorbance in the absence of Zn/NB-CDs, and ODsample represents the absorbance in the presence of Zn/NB-CDs.

The bio-labeling potential of Zn/NB-CDs and Zn/NB-CDs/Co2+ solutions were preliminarily assessed through cellular imaging in HCT-116 cells. Zn/NB-CDs and Zn/NB-CDs/Co2+ tagged HCT-116 cells were detected by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was performed using an IX-81 Olympus CLSM microscope (Japan). Images were captured at an excitation and emission wavelength of 370 and 451 nm.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of Zn/NB-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1 a, exhibit a minor absorption peak at 292 nm, which is ascribed to the π–π* transition taking place in the framework of graphite sp2 configuration. Additionally, a broader absorption peak is noticed at 380 nm, corresponding to the n–π* transition of surface state defects in the Zn/NB-CDs, introduced by the co-doping of nitrogen and boron, and the doping of zinc metal. The Zn/NB-CDs also exhibit maximum excitation and emission peaks at 370 nm and 451 nm, correspondingly as shown in Fig. 1b. Furthermore, the water dispersion of Zn/NB-CDs demonstrates excitation dependent activity, changing from 300 to 400 nm, indicating a uniform surface state and size distribution, in the peak excitation level perceived at 370 nm (Fig. 1c). The emission wavelength and fluorescence intensity are depending on excitation, which has potential for multimodal imaging applications. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the Zn/NB-CDs was 25.10 % when excited at 370 nm, and their emission peak wavelength was 451 nm [33]. The differences in Zn/NB-CDs material sizes and surface state distribution may be responsible for the fluctuation in excitation-sensitive behaviour.

The pH value significantly influences the sensing performance of materials. To investigate this effect, the sensing behavior of Zn/NB-CDs for Co2+ ions was studied by monitoring the fluorescence (FL) intensity changes at the maximum emission wavelength. Fig. 2 a illustrates the FL intensity of Zn/NB-CDs across a pH range of 1–13 [34,35]. In the absence of Co2+ ions, the FL intensity of Zn/NB-CDs increases under acidic conditions as the pH rises, reaching a peak at pH ∼ 7.0, and then decreases under alkaline conditions. Upon the addition of Co2+ ions, significant FL enhancing is observed at all pH values, indicating a strong interaction between the ions and Zn/NB-CDs. These FL changes are likely caused by the protonation of NH2 groups in highly low pH conditions and the accompanying deprotonation of OH groups in highly alkaline conditions. These processes disrupt the aromatic conjugated system and the overall complex structure, leading to altered FL properties. The observed pH range for sensing is broader than that of typical physiological environments, suggesting potential suitability for in vivo applications.

Based on these findings, pH ∼ 7.0 was selected as the optimal condition for conducting subsequent Co2+ sensing experiments. The impact of electrolyte concentration on the fluorescence intensity of Zn/NB-CDs was investigated using fluorescence spectroscopy. Specifically, Zn/NB-CDs were dissolved in NaCl solutions [36], Zn/NB-CDs ranging from 0.2 M to 1.0 M as shown in Fig. 2b. Notably, the fluorescence intensity remained relatively stable across this concentration range, indicating that the optical properties of Zn/NB-CDs are insensitive to changes in ionic strength. This robustness ensures consistent performance in diverse environmental conditions, making Zn/NB-CDs suitable for various applications. Further characterization of Zn/NB-CDs optical properties involved measuring their absorption peak a 365 nm under both UV light and daylight illumination as shown in Fig. S3. This assessment aimed to detect any alterations in the emission spectrum or intensity of Zn/NB-CDs under varying illumination conditions. This suggests that Zn/NB-CDs are remarkably resistant to radiation, which might otherwise cause chemical or physical changes in materials. Their capacity to maintain optical properties under these conditions highlights their durability, rendering them suitable for radiation-sensitive applications. Similarly, the FL intensity of Zn/NB-CDs showed only a slight decrease even after 90 days of storage (Fig. 2c), demonstrating their long-lasting performance [37]. This minimal fluorescence intensity changes in optical properties over time is vital for effective applications that demand stable behavior, like in sensors or imaging devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological properties of Zn/NB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Zn/NB-CDs were thoroughly characterized by means of various analytical approaches, such as FT-IR, XRD, TEM and XPS. Fig. 3 a illustrates the FT-IR spectroscopy analysis of Zn/NB-CDs, which was conducted to investigate the functional groups present on their surface. A broad absorption band at around 3400 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH groups. The peaks at 3310 cm−1 corresponded to NH stretching frequencies, while absorptions near 3010 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1 were associated with alkyl CH stretching and bending vibrations, respectively. A peak at 1308 cm−1, 1088 cm−1, and 961 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum for Zn/NB-CDs corresponds to BO, BOH, and CB whereas the peaks at 1700 cm−1 and 1536 cm−1 indicated the presence of CO and CN stretching vibrations. These observations suggest the presence of functional groups such as OH, CH, and CO on the surface of the Zn/NB-CDs. Additionally, the peaks at 1521 cm−1 and 1150 cm−1 were attributed to CC and CBC vibrations, indicating aromatic structures within the Zn/NB-CDs in the metal-sensitive region (400–600 cm−1), a distinct peak at 527 cm−1 indicated the presence of OZnO stretching vibrations [38], confirming interactions similar to those observed in Zn/NB-CDs. These FT-IR findings confirm the successful doping of Zn with N and B co-doped into the Zn/NB-CDs, enhancing their structural properties.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis is a crucial technique used to determine the crystalline structure of carbon dots (CDs). In the context of carbon dots, XRD provides insights into their graphitic and amorphous characteristics. Typically, the XRD pattern of CDs shows broad diffraction peaks that correspond to graphitic carbon structures. A common peak observed around 22° (2θ) is usually associated with the (002) [39] plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. 3b), signifying the presence of sp2-hybridized carbon with a certain degree of crystallinity indicating layered graphitic domains within the CDs [40]. This peak suggests that the carbon atoms are arranged in a manner similar to graphite, though often less ordered due to nanoscale size and potential defects within the structure. The sharpness and intensity of these peaks can vary depending on the degree of graphitization, doping elements, or functionalization present on the carbon dots.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is a vital technique for characterizing the morphology and size of carbon dots (Zn/NB-CDs) at the nanoscale level. The TEM images often show uniformity in particle size, indicating consistent formation of the carbon dots. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) goes a step further by allowing the visualization of lattice fringes within the Zn/NB-CDs, which can confirm the presence of graphitic or crystalline domains. These fringes, typically with spacings of around 0.21 nm (Fig. 4 a), correspond to the (002) planes of graphitic carbon, suggesting partial graphitization within the carbon dots [41]. The average diameter of the Zn/NB-CDs was found to be 3.0 ± 0.2 nm as shown in Fig. 4b, demonstrating a tight size distribution, as confirmed by a rigorous statistical analysis of 100 particles, highlighting their outstanding size uniformity.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the surface composition and chemical bonds of the Zn/NB-CDs. The XPS spectrum revealed five prominent peaks at 1025.7, 192.6, 400.1, 284.3, and 531.6 eV, corresponding to Zn2p, B1s, N1s, C1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 5 a). Elemental analysis indicated a composition of 18.14 % Zn2p, 14.03 % B1s, 21.82 % N1s, 37.36 % C1s, and 8.65 % O1s. In the Zn2p XPS spectrum, there are two main peaks:

Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2 peak at 1021.3 and 1044.3 eV (Fig. 5b). However, in some cases, additional peaks may be observed in the Zn2p XPS spectrum, such as: A shoulder or satellite peak at around 1024–1026 eV, which can be attributed to ZnO or ZnN bonds. A peak at around 1017–1019 eV and 1050–1052 eV, which can be attributed to ZnC or ZnB and ZnOH bonds [19]. The N1s expanded spectrum showed four distinct carbon states: ZnN, CNC, NC, and NH, at 397.4, 399.6, 401.3, and 401.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 5c). Deconvolution of the B1s spectrum (Fig. 5d) yielded three peaks at 190.4, 191.2, and 192.3 eV, attributed to BC, BO, and BZn bonds, respectively [42]. The C1s spectrum revealed three peaks at 283.8, 284.7, and 286.2 eV, 287.1 eV corresponding to CC, CO, CN, and CZn bonds, respectively (Fig. 5e). Further, the O1s spectrum showed (Fig. 5f) three peaks at 531.0, 531.7, and 532.6 eV, attributed to CO, CO, and OCO groups. These findings, in agreement with FT-IR results, confirmed the effective integration of Zn, N and B into the CDs, with the presence of functional groups such as amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence responses of Zn/NB-CDs were accurately examined against a variety of metal ions, including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ce3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Sr2+, Zr2+, and Zn2+ at 10 μM concentration to Zn/NB-CDs solutions (5 μg/mL), upon the addition of Co2+, the absorption of the band at 264 nm was enhanced (Fig. S1a). As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, only Co2+ caused notable luminous alterations, showcasing the superior affinity of Zn/NB-CDs toward this distinct ion. The fluorescence spectrum exhibited a significant improvement in the emission peak at 451 nm as a result of the addition of Co2+. In addition to metal ion detection, fluorescence sensing may also be used for Co2+ ion detection, as demonstrated in the following example. Significantly, the intensity of FL increased when the Co2+ ion was added. Zn/NB-CDs potential as a FL probe for Co2+ ion detection was demonstrated by the progressive rise of their FL intensity at 451 nm with increasing Co2+ ion concentrations. Visual evidence of this concentration-dependent reaction was provided by the inset photos (Fig. 6b), which show how the addition of Co2+ ions changed the mild blue emission of Zn/NB-CDs into a vivid blue emission. The fluorescence color shift in Zn/NB-CDs solutions upon exposure to different metal ions under 365 nm UV light confirms that Zn/NB-CDs can detect metal ions efficiently thanks to their distinct fluorescence properties. Using Zn/NB-CDs for quantitative detection of Co2+ ions under ideal circumstances was extensively studied. An aqueous solution of Zn/NB-CDs with and without Co2+ is shown to vary visually under 365 nm UV light in Fig. 6c. The addition of Co2+ caused the fluorescence intensity to change from light blue to brilliant blue, indicating a significant increase in fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity increased exponentially when stimulated at 370 nm, and this augmentation was associated with the quantity of Co2+. Co2+ ions and the reactive sites on the Zn/NB-CDs surface are thought to coordinate to produce the observed blueshift in emission wavelength, which is explained by charge transfer between the Zn/NB-CDs and Co2+ ions. Fig. 7 a shows an improved efficiency, 1/[F − Fo], plotted against the change in fluorescence intensity at 451 nm. This efficiency showed a good linear connection (R2 = 0.9964) with concentrations of Co2+ ions ranging from 0 to 10 μM [43]. Quantitative Co2+ ion detection was made possible by this linear connection. According to Fig. 7b, the equation LOD = 3 × SD/S was used to determine the notable limit of detection (LOD) for Co2+, which came out to be 3.37 nM. With low detection limits and good sensitivity, Zn/NB-CDs may detect Co2+ in aqueous conditions, according to this value. The linear operating range of 0–10 μM was shown by the limit of detection (LOD), which was found to be 3.37 nM. These results point to possible uses for Zn/NB-CDs in the detection of Co2+ ions, which might help meet drinking water quality regulations established by agencies like the US Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA). Because zinc doped, nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots (Zn/NB-CDs) can detect ions selectively even when there are several competing cations present, their success as sensors for Co2+ ion detection is mostly based on this property. To clarify the fluorescence-enhancing mechanism for Co2+ sensing, more research was conducted. Although the exact mechanism is still being investigated, the enhancing process is mostly dependent on the interaction between Co2+ ions and the chemically active regions on the surface of Zn/NB-CDs. Using a Job’s plot analysis, the binding ratio between Zn/NB-CDs and Co2+ was examined (Fig. 7c). An analysis was conducted by plotting the fluorescence intensity at 451 nm versus the molar ratio of [Co2+] to [Co2+ + Zn/NB-CDs]. According to the findings, a molar ratio of 0.5 was consistently associated with the highest FL intensity. A 1:1 stoichiometric ratio between Zn/NB-CDs and Co2+ ions was verified by Job’s plot analysis, confirming the formation of a stable chelate.

To evaluate this selectivity, the fluorescence (FL) response of Zn/NB-CDs was assessed after treatment with 1 µM of Co2+ ions, together with equivalent concentrations (1 µM) of several potential interfering metal ions, including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ce3+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Sr2+, Zr2+, and Zn2+. This experiment was designed to simulate real-world in environmentally and biologically relevant scenarios, involving multiple coexisting metal ions. As illustrated in Figs. S2a, the FL intensity of Zn/NB-CDs was significantly enhanced in the presence of Co2+ ions, even when other metal cations were present. This substantial enhancing effect demonstrates the sensor exceptional selectivity toward Co2+ ions. The selective and strong coordination of metal ions to functional groups on the surface of Zn/NB-CDs likely involving mechanisms such as complexation or charge transfer enables this selective quenching. This is especially crucial because Co2+ ions often be present simultaneously with other metal ions in different environments and can be challenging to detect resulting from their similar ionic radii and charges [44]. Conversely, this reinforces the high selectivity of Zn/NB-CDs for Co2+ ions, as other cations do not interfere with the detection process. The negligible impact of competing ions highlights the sensor ability to maintain sensitivity and specificity in complex mixtures, which is vital for effective sensing technologies. The FL histogram data and fluorescence colour change (Fig. S2b and S2c) further support the sensor capability to distinguish Co2+ ions in the presence of other cations with excellent selectivity. The ability of Zn/NB-CDs to enhance emission of fluorescence upon exposure to Co2+ ions, with sustained accuracy even in complex mixtures containing competing species, underscores the sensor sensitivity and specificity. This is particularly important for applications in biological environments (such as human physiological conditions) or environmental monitoring, where detecting metal ion contamination or imbalance is crucial. The performance of the sensor in PBS buffer solutions (pH 7.4) also emphasizes its suitability for biological applications, as this pH is physiologically relevant. The combination of high selectivity and excellent sensitivity makes Zn/NB-CDs well-suited for detecting Co2+ ions in a range of sample types, from water samples to biological fluids. Due to their strong reliability in complex environments, Zn/NB-CDs hold great potential for various applications. They can be effectively used in environmental monitoring to detect heavy metal pollutants, aid in diagnosing diseases linked to metal ion imbalances, and support industrial processes that require accurate metal ion detection.

A fluorescence (FL) retrieval study was carried out to evaluate the effectiveness of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) in restoring the FL intensity of zinc doped, nitrogen and boron-codoped carbon dots (Zn/NB-CDs) that had been enhanced by Co2+ ions. The chelating properties of EDTA were exploited by adding it at 10 µM to determine its effectiveness in restoring FL. As depicted in Fig. 8 a, EDTA treatment resulted in a pronounced recovery of FL intensity, achieving the highest level of restoration. The addition of EDTA resulted in a remarkable FL recovery exceeding 90 % when Co2+ ions augmented the fluorescence of Zn/NB-CDs [45]. EDTA's ability to form a stable complex with Co2+ ions is responsible for the observed recovery, as it effectively binds to and removes the ions from the Zn/NB-CDs surface, thus disrupting the enhancement. The enhancing, which likely involved electronic interactions or resonance energy transfer between Co2+ ions and the chemically active groups on the Zn/NB-CDs, is reversed, allowing the FL of the Zn/NB-CDs to be restored. The selective FL intensity recovery enabled by EDTA for Co2+ ions suggest a hypothetical mechanism involving metal ions. This phenomenon is evident from both the pronounced FL intensity changes and the accompanying color shifts, as illustrated in Fig. 8b. The formation of EDTA-metal ion complexes with high stability, along with the ability to reverse the FL enhancing, allows this recovery process that can be cycled numerous times, showcasing the effectiveness of EDTA in restoring the fluorescence of Zn/NB-CDs.

Smartphones are turning into useful instruments for analyte detection by interpreting chromogenic reactions through digital picture processing. A practical and effective way to gather and process data is provided by this strategy. The sophisticated color detecting features of contemporary smartphones enable them to precisely measure even the smallest color changes. Through the use of RGB color pickers, these devices evaluate colors according to the intensity levels of the 8-bit resolution red, green, and blue (RGB) channels. Colors in digital photographs are represented by pixel intensity values, which range from 255 (brightest) to 0 (darkest). In a recent work, Zn/NB-CDs and Zn/NB-CDs/Co2+ aqueous solutions were photographed using a Vivo Y1907 AI triple-camera smartphone at 365 nm under UV light [46]. The RGB values were then extracted and quantified from the solutions using a color picker tool (Fig. 9 ). While the Zn/NB-CDs/Co2+ solution looked bright blue with RGB values of (50, 155, 295), the Zn/NB-CDs solution showed a light blue hue with RGB values of (5, 22, 140). This technique offers a rapid and convenient solution for onsite visual analysis, allowing users to efficiently determine RGB values through smartphone applications. The adoption of smartphone-based colorimetry greatly improves the accessibility and effectiveness of colorimetric assays across various domains, such as environmental monitoring and medical diagnostics. By streamlining the detection process, this technology enhances the practicality and adaptability of color analysis for real-world applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid detection of Co2+ using test strip
ce:section.ce:para: A series of experiments were carried out using paper strip with Whatman filter paper to assess the potential applications of the Zn/NB-CDs chemosensor probe. A stock solution of Zn/NB-CDs was to prepare along with various metal cation solutions. To perform the tests, paper strips were first dipped into the Zn/NB-CDs solution and then exposed to analytes at room temperature, including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ce3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Sr2+, Zr2+, and Zn2+. When the Zn/NB-CDs-coated strips were illuminated under UV light at 365 nm, they emitted a light blue fluorescence (Fig. 10 a). Interestingly, the addition of most cations did not result in any significant color changes [47]. However, when Co2+ ions were introduced, there was a noticeable ratiometric color shift from light blue to bright blue (Fig. 10b). This distinct and immediate response highlights the high sensitivity of the Zn/NB-CDs for detecting Co2+ ions. The potential for creating quick and accurate test strips especially for Co2+ ion analysis in a range of contexts is demonstrated by this straightforward and efficient detection technique. The clear visual shift and ease of use make this approach a promising tool for applications in environmental monitoring, quality control, and other fields requiring sensitive ion detection.

Zn/NB-CDs were used as a chemosensor to determine the cobalt ion concentration of figs, cabbage, and pista [48]. In particular, we looked into the amount of Co2+ in dietary samples, including figs, cabbage, and pista. A 100 mg sample of freshly crushed figs, cabbage, and pista (Fresh market Private Ltd., India) was dissolved in 10 mL of double-distilled water. After that, the Zn/NB-CDs solution was mixed with 10 µM of this extract solution. The resultant mixture was examined using fluorescence spectroscopy, which showed emission peaks at 451 nm (Fig. 11 a). Along with this spectral shift, there was a visible color change in the Zn/NB-CDs from light blue to bright blue upon adding the food samples (Fig. 11b). These results demonstrate that the Zn/NB-CDs chemosensor is highly effective in detecting and quantifying Co2+ ions in food sample formulations. The newly devised protocol facilitates a rapid and dependable assessment of cobalt concentrations in edible commodities such as figs, cabbage, and pista, thereby constituting a crucial resource for ensuring culinary product integrity.

The quantitative information to confirm the Zn/NB-CDs chemosensors performance in the quantification and detection of Co2+ ions in real food samples, including figs, cabbage, and pista. The samples were spiked with 1 µM, 5 µM, and 10 µM levels of Co2+ with Zn/NB-CDs. From the calibration curve, we obtained the corresponding Co2+ concentrations and calculated the recovery rates and RSD values. These results are summarized in Table S4, which demonstrates that the sensor shows good accuracy recoveries range from 95 % to 105 % and the relative standard deviation (RSD) for each sample was less than 4.5 % across all tested food matrices.

The compatibility with living systems and promising implementation of zinc doped with nitrogen and boron-codoped carbon dots (Zn/NB-CDs) in living organisms were evaluated through an in vitro cytotoxicity assay using HCT-116 cancer cells [49]. Biocompatibility was evaluated using the MTT cells were subjected to treatment and analyzed with various volume fractions of Zn/NB-CDs spanning 0 to 100 μg/mL for 24 h. As illustrated in Fig. 12 a, cell viability was maintained stable across all concentration range, exhibiting a value below 5 % repletion in viability, revealing minimal toxicity. The minimal decrement observed at elevated concentrations confirms the excellent compatibility with biological systems of Zn/NB-CDs, rendering them ideal for biomedical uses such as cell imaging and labeling. To further explore their use in bioimaging, optical microscopy analysis was conducted with HCT-116 cancer cells. As shown in Fig. 12b, cells treated with Zn/NB-CDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence when excited at 451 nm, consistent with the Zn/NB-CDs successfully demonstrated membrane permeability and intracellular penetration. Moreover, upon exposure of cells to Zn/NB-CDs with the addition of Co2+ ions, a significant enhancing of luminescence was observed. This demonstrates that Zn/NB-CDs can selectively measure intracellular metal ion accumulation by altering their fluorescence intensity. The capacity for selective recognition of Co2+ ions highlight the potential of Zn/NB-CDs as effective fluorescent probes for cellular metal ion imaging. The results suggest that Zn/NB-CDs are a promising tool for in vitro cell imaging and labeling, owing to their favorable imaging performance and low cytotoxicity. Their ability to efficiently penetrate cells, emit strong fluorescence, and exhibit high biocompatibility suggesting their potential as valuable assets in biomedical research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative determination of Co2+ ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of Zn/NB-CDs as a chemical detector the accuracy of Co2+ ions detection in environmental water samples was assessed through a standard addition method. Tap, sea, and well water samples were intentionally corrupted by adding standard concentrations of Co2+ ions [50]. The fluorescent signal strengths of these specimens were quantified and correlated with the reference calibration graph established for Co2+ ions, spanning concentrations from 0 to 10 μM.

The enhancing of luminescent signals detected in water analytes enabled the evaluation of metal ion amounts. The precision of this detection approach used assessed via recovery efficiency calculation of the added ions in the aqueous analytes. The percentage recoveries of Co2+ ions were between 99.0 % to 111.1 %, with a high relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.39 %, highlighting the accuracy and consistency of the detection technique (Table 1 ) [51]. The recovery values nearing 100 % underscore the Zn/NB-CDs sensor’s capability for precise detection and quantification of Co2+ ions, even in complex environmental water samples. Successful application tap, sea, and well water samples demonstrate the potential of Zn/NB-CDs for effective monitoring of heavy metal contamination in real-world environmental settings.

In conclusion, the synthesized zinc doped with nitrogen and boron-codoped carbon dots (Zn/NB-CDs) exhibit outstanding their distinctive optical and structural characteristics make them highly effective for detecting Co2+ ions and imaging cancer cells. They exhibit strong UV–vis absorption peaks at 292 nm and broad absorption peak at 380 nm, suggesting notable electronic transitions influenced by heteroatom doping. This doping effect enhances their fluorescence properties, yielding intense emission with excitation and emission maxima at 370 nm and 451 nm, respectively. The resulting Zn/NB-CDs demonstrated several impressive properties, including a narrow size distribution and a high quantum yield of 25.10 %, significantly surpassing the low quantum yield of non-metal-doped CDs. Stability tests indicate that the Zn/NB-CDs they retain 94 % of their original fluorescence intensity even after 30 min of radiation exposure and exhibit minimal degradation over 90 days, highlighting their exceptional stability. Elemental analysis indicates a composition of 25.06 % zinc, 7.24 % nitrogen, and 4.11 % boron, which contributes to the presence of diverse functional groups and enhances their overall functionality. The excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission observed in the Zn/NB-CDs can be attributed to their narrow size distribution, with small particle sizes of 3.0 ± 0.2 nm, and their high crystallinity. Furthermore, Zn/NB-CDs exhibit remarkable sensitivity and selectivity in detecting Co2+ ions, as demonstrated by their significant fluorescence enhancement upon interaction with these ions. They achieve an impressive detection limit as low as 3.37 nM. Linear regression analysis shows a strong correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9964 for Co2+) across a concentration range of 0–10 µM, confirming their reliability for precise metal ion quantification. The feasible applications have been investigated the sensing platform realizes the detection of Co2+ in figs, cabbage, and pista food samples with satisfactory FL recovery of over 90 %. Zn/NB-CDs exhibit outstanding biocompatibility and minimal cytotoxicity, as evidenced by MTT assay results. These results demonstrate that the viability of HCT-116 cancer cells remains above 95 % across a concentration range of 0 to 100 μg/mL. In environmental applications, Zn/NB-CDs have proven to be highly effective in metal ion quantification, achieving remarkable accuracy. The recovery rates for spiked Co2+ ions range between 99.0 % and 111.1 %, with an impressively low relative standard deviation of 2.39 %. These findings highlight the versatility of Zn/NB-CDs, making them valuable for both biomedical research and environmental monitoring. Their potential extends to advanced imaging techniques and the precise detection of heavy metals, further enhancing their application in scientific and industrial fields.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Alagarsamy Priyadharshini: She received her B.Sc. (2021) and M.Sc. (2023) degree in Chemistry at Madurai Kamaraj University, India. Now she is doing doctoral research under the supervision of Prof. Dr. A. A. Napoleon at the Department of Chemistry, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, India. Her research focuses on the design and synthesis of small molecules-derived carbon nanomaterials for sensor applications, including the selective detection of metal ions, real sample analysis, and live cell imaging.

Ayyakannu Arumugam Napoleon: Dr. A.A. Napoleon is an avid researcher and Professor at VIT University, Vellore. He has completed his Ph.D. in 2015 under the guidance of Dr. F. Nawaz Khan, from Department of Chemistry, VIT University, Vellore on the topic of “Studies on synthesis, biological evaluations of few nitrogen and oxygen heterocycles (pyrazolines, isochromen-1- ones, xanthenediones). He has completed his master degree M.Pharm from Birla Institute of Technology in 1997 and Bachelor degree B.Pharm from The Tamilnadu Dr MGR Medical University, Chennai, India in 1994. He has published over 60 Research papers with good impact factor journal publications. His current research work is primarily focuses on the development of probe as Chemosensor for metal ion sensing applications, environmental, and live cell imaging and developing Carbon Nanomaterials-Carbon dots, Also designing novel heterocyclic compounds and exploring for suitable pharmacological applications from synthetic medicinal chemistry and from natural products.
